coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-modified screen-printed electrode for the determination of cannabidiol (CBD) in hemp seeds flour
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abid, Khouloud
Ferlazzo, Angelo
Neri, Giovanni
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the development of a graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-modified screen-printed carbon (SPCE) electrode for the determination of cannabidiol (CBD), a cannabinoid present in the Cannabis L. Sativa (hemp) plant, is reported. This cannabinoid is non-intoxicating and non-psychoactive, thus CBD-containing drugs as well as CBD-containing foods, are now on the market in many countries due to their health-beneficial effects and pharmacological activities. Because of the increasing interest in analyzing CBD in these real samples, we analyzed here a sample of hemp flour that was used for testing the developed CBD electrochemical sensor. Preliminary work devoted to optimizing the analysis conditions, allowed to development of a GQDs-modified electrode with promising characteristics for the simple screening of CBD. The electroanalytical tests for CBD detection showed a sensitivity of 0.98 μAμM-1 cm−2, which is increased by 2-folder compared to bare SPCE, and a limit of detection (LOD) equal to 0.277 μM. The developed GQDs/SPCE sensor and the analysis procedure were then applied for the CBD analysis in a hemp seeds flour sample. Based on the results obtained, the advantages/disadvantages evidenced by operating with the developed electrochemical sensor in the analysis of CBD in real samples were discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical sensor
Screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE)
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Cannabidiol (CBD)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) synthesis and characterization
CBD standard and extraction from hemp seed
Electrochemical optimization of GQDs/SPCE for CBD determination
Electrochemical screening of flour seeds sample with GQDs/SPCE

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Consuming rate of CBD in different countries in Europe.
(a) Molecular structure and (b) 3D conformer of cannabidiol (CBD).
CBD electrochemical sensors based on the Scopus database from 2014 to the present.
Representative scheme describing the extraction procedure of hemp seeds flour.

A) XRD spectrum of precursor go; b) XRD spectrum of synthesized gqds; c) raman spectrum of synthesized gqds; d) dls pattern of synthesized gqds. inset shows a representative tem image of one GQDs particle of 3.4 nm in diameter.
ATR_FTIR spectra of GO (black line) and GQDs (red line).

(a) CV and (b) EIS tests bare SPCE and GQDs/SPCE electrodes in 10 mM [FeCN6]4-/3− solution. Inset the equivalent circuit. (c) Scan rate variation of GQDs/SPCE in [FeCN6]4-/3− solution and (d) the variation of scan rate.
DPV tests for (a) bare SPCE and (b) GQDs/SPCE in PBS with different pH.
DPV tests for (a) bare SPCE and (b) GQDs/SPCE in PBS with different concentrations of CBD.

(a) Electrocatalytic process of CBD oxidation on the GQDs-modified electrode surface. b) Calibration curve for the SPCE and GQDs/SPCE electrodes in PBS (0.01 M, pH = 5) containing different concentrations of CBD (SD ≤ 0. 17 for 3 time).
Reproducibility test of the GQDs/SPCE electrode at 6.4 μM of CBD (SD ≤ 0. 12 for 3 time).

A) DPV response recorded with the hemp seeds flour sample; b) calibration curves extracted by dpv tests carried out with gqds/spce in the presence of cbd in ethanol and with cbd added to hemp flour extract.
Test carried out spiking CBD in the solution of Hemp seeds flour sample.
Randles parameters of the unmodified and modified electrode.
Comparison of the sensor performances between our sensor and previous ones in the literature.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-modified screen-printed electrode for the determination of cannabidiol (CBD) in hemp seeds flour

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering, University of Messina, C. da Di Doi, I-98166 Messina, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Engineering, University of Messina, C. da Di Doi, I-98166 Messina, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Sciences, University of Catania, Viale A. Doria 6, 95125 Catania, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Sciences, University of Catania, Viale A. Doria 6, 95125 Catania, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the development of a graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-modified screen-printed carbon (SPCE) electrode for the determination of cannabidiol (CBD), a cannabinoid present in the Cannabis L. Sativa (hemp) plant, is reported. This cannabinoid is non-intoxicating and non-psychoactive, thus CBD-containing drugs as well as CBD-containing foods, are now on the market in many countries due to their health-beneficial effects and pharmacological activities. Because of the increasing interest in analyzing CBD in these real samples, we analyzed here a sample of hemp flour that was used for testing the developed CBD electrochemical sensor. Preliminary work devoted to optimizing the analysis conditions, allowed to development of a GQDs-modified electrode with promising characteristics for the simple screening of CBD. The electroanalytical tests for CBD detection showed a sensitivity of 0.98 μAμM-1 cm−2, which is increased by 2-folder compared to bare SPCE, and a limit of detection (LOD) equal to 0.277 μM. The developed GQDs/SPCE sensor and the analysis procedure were then applied for the CBD analysis in a hemp seeds flour sample. Based on the results obtained, the advantages/disadvantages evidenced by operating with the developed electrochemical sensor in the analysis of CBD in real samples were discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cannabis, commonly referred to as marijuana, is a prominent psychoactive substance that has been extensively consumed around the world with an expected market nearly 68€ billion in the next 4 years [1]. More than 150 million (more than 3.5 %) of the population consume marijuana [2]. In 2019, Italy and France have shown a huge increase in consumption rate compared to 2013, see Fig. 1 .

Cannabis can be consumed in various ways including drinking tea, smoking, and eating. Within the spectrum of over 140 phytocannabinoids found in the Cannabis sativa L (C. Sativa) plant [1,3], Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol (Δ9-THC) and cannabidiol (CBD) stand out as the most widely recognized substances [1,3]. According to the literature, the first cannabinoid extracted from this plant was CBD, with its molecular and conformer structure depicted in Fig. 2 .

While cannabis consumption is generally deemed illegal, several countries have legalized its consumption to solve depression problems and analgesic issues [1]. Moreover, it is used as an anti-inflammatory as well as an anxiety treatment, and neuroprotective [3–5]. Nevertheless, its abuse leads to addiction and other issues. Thus, it is crucial to determine CBD content to prevent its selling and promotion in secret and on the black market as well as its smuggling.

CBD determination can be carried out by various techniques. Among them, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), mass spectroscopy (MS), and gas chromatography (GC) are the most routinely used [6–8]. Despite their high sensitivity and selectivity, these techniques are time-consuming, require an expert researcher to conduct the analysis, and are high cost. To overcome these limitations, electrochemical sensors have been proposed for the determination of CBD (see Fig. 3 ). These techniques possess numerous pros denoting the sensitivity and selectivity allowing for the detection of even trace levels and in the presence of other interfering substances, respectively. Moreover, these tools are known for their real-time and fast response that can be provided in a few minutes or seconds. Therefore, electrochemical techniques have a great potential for application for the determination of a variety of analytes [9,10]. Among these, we can cite many clinical biomarkers like uric acid and ascorbic acid [11], folic acid [12], tyrosine [13], dopamine [14,15], and environmental pollutants e.g., mangiferin [16], thiram [17], heavy metals [18], and organic compounds such as nitrobenzene [19]. Anyway, according to the literature, electrochemical detection of CBD is still limited and it is based on glassy carbon electrodes or graphite electrodes using carbon derivatives as modifiers (mainly black carbon and carbon nanotubes) [20].

In this work, we reported a study on the determination of CBD by using an electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which consist of a few graphene sheets smaller than 100 nm. Due to their interesting properties, such as high surface area, low cytotoxicity, appropriate conductivity, and synthesis from abundant raw materials of low cost, GQDs have been explored in several areas of research including potential application in electrochemistry. Graphene oxide (GO) is the most usual two-dimensional layered nanostructure used to synthesize graphene quantum dots. The modification of SPCE by using GQDs has been utilized in the development of electrochemical sensors for the detection of various analytes [21–23]. This outstanding interest in the use of GQDs is owing to their features denoting the large surface area, edge effect, and quantum confinement [24], coupled with a semiconductor behavior [21]. However, to our knowledge, this is the first time that a graphene quantum dots-modified screen-printed carbon electrode is used as a CBD electrochemical sensor.

Since the interest in CBD-related products is growing, the simple and rapid detection of this molecule, combined with the design of a small and portable sensing probe, could meet many needs not only in forensic science or clinical toxicology but also in agricultural, dietary, and food. Indeed, due to the high nutritional value of hemp seeds, the interest in the food industry began to grow [25]. Hemp can be used as an adjunct in the production of functional foods, however, there are no specific studies and/or indications on the quantities allowed for human consumption is still a matter of discussion. The content of CBD in the Hemp depends on the parts of the plant considered, and it is higher in the leaves. Although hemp seeds are CBD-free, the flour can be accidentally contaminated by this substance coming from leaves [26]. Therefore, the effort to develop simple electrochemical sensors for CBD analysis in hemp seed flour is nowadays highly demanding.

All chemicals, H2SO4, HNO3, NaOH, GO were obtained from commercial sources (Merk Life Science S.r.l., Milan, Italy). Cannabidiol (Cerilliant®) used in the analyses had a concentration of 1.0 mg/mL in methanol, ethanol (>98) was purchased from Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany, distilled water was produced by an in-house distiller.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) synthesis and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs have been prepared as follows [17]: 300 mg of graphene oxide (GO) was treated with 100 mL of an oxidizing acid mixture (HNO3/H2SO4 1:3 ratio). The reaction flask equipped with a condenser was immersed in an ultrasonic bath maintaining the temperature at 60 °C for 4 days. Afterward, the suspension was diluted with deionized water and filtered using a 0.1 μm Millipore membrane. The filtrate was neutralized with a NaOH solution until a neutral pH was reached and was purified using dialysis bags (12 KDa) until reaction residues were completely removed in the wash water. The purified GQDs are then stored and kept in suspension to avoid aggregation processes.

The morphological and microstructural characteristics of the prepared GQDs were investigated by several complementary techniques. X-ray diffraction (XRD) XRD analysis was performed on a D2 Phaser Bruker diffractometer equipped with a Ni β-filtered Cu-Kα radiation source, operating at 30 kV and 10 mA in the range 20–80° (2-theta), at a scanning rate of 0.025° s − 1. Photoluminescence (PL) analyses were performed using a spectrofluorometer NanoLog modular (Horiba, Ltd.), under excitation with a xenon lamp; the nanomaterials water dispersions were analyzed at the concentration of 100 ng/mL exciting the samples at the fixed wavelength of 360 nm. ATR-FTIR analyses were performed using a Spectrum Two (Perkin-Elmer) FT-IR spectrometer with a diamond ATR (Attenuated Total Reflection) single-reflection accessory.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CBD standard and extraction from hemp seed
ce:section.ce:para: Cannabidiol (1,0 mg/mL in methanol, Cerilliant®) has been used as standard. Reagent grade ethanol (EtOH, Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany) and distilled water were used for the extraction of CBD from hemp seeds flour, supplied by Molino Crisafulli (Caltagirone, Italy), obtained by stone milling of hemp seed after cold pressing and sieving. For the CBD extraction, hemp flour was mixed with 25 mL of pure ethanol (EtOH) and ultrasonicated for 15 min. After that, we agitated the obtained solution for 24 h at 700 rpm. Once it was finished, we filtered the obtained mixture to obtain 25 mL of hemp seed flour, see Fig. 4 . The extraction method was validated by repeating the procedure three times and confirming the composition by chromatographic analysis.

Gas chromatography (GC) analyses on the hemp seeds extract were carried out on a GCMS-QP2020 instrument (Shimadzu, Duisburg, Germany) equipped with a split-splitless injector (280 ◦C) and an AOC-20i autosampler. Separations were performed on a SLB-5 ms [silphenylene polymer with similar polarity to poly (5 % diphenyl/95 % dimethyl siloxane)] 30 m × 0.25 mm I.D. × 0.25 μm d.f. capillary column Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany), and the temperature program was as follows: from 50 ◦C to 360 ◦C at 3.0 ◦C/min. The injection volume was 0.5 μL with a split ratio of 1:50. Helium was employed as a carrier gas, with a constant linear velocity of 30 cm/s and an initial inlet pressure of 26.6 kPa.

Electrochemical tests were performed using a commercial screen-printed carbon three-electrode platform. PBS (10 mmol/L, pH 5.0, containing 10 % methanol) was used as a solvent and supporting electrolyte. The GQDs modified electrochemical sensor GQDs/SPCE, were fabricated dropping 30 µL of GQDs on the working electrode of SPCE.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used for the measurement, with a scan rate of 0.05 V s−1 and a potential range from 0 V to 0.8 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was applied to characterize the sensor conductivity in the solution containing 5.0 mmolL-1 of K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] and 0.1 molL-1 of potassium chloride. The amplitude was 0.005 V with a frequency range of 0.1 to 105 Hz. All experiments were carried out in three duplicates at 25 °C.

Electroanalytical tests were performed by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique under the optimized variables: Estep = 0.016 V, Epuls = 0.1 V, tpuls = 100 ms, and Srate = 0.05 V/s in the potential range [0 V-1 V].

GQDs can be synthesized by many different carbon nanostructures used as raw materials [27]. GQDs used in this study were synthesized from graphene oxide GO. The detailed synthesis and characterization of GQDs synthesized from GO have been reported in a previous paper [16]. Here, we briefly discuss the key characteristics of the GQDs which are important in the development of the electrochemical sensor for CBD determination.

Fig. 5 a,b shows the results obtained performing XRD characterization for both GO and GQDs samples. XRD pattern of GO depicts an intense peak at ∼27-2θ degrees and another smaller and larger peak at ∼44-2θ degrees. On the GQDs sample, XRD revealed a large peak at ∼28-2θ, [22], indicating the successful transformation of the crystalline GO into an almost amorphous and/or nanometer-sized GQDs.

Raman spectrum of the synthesized GQDs is shown in Fig. 5c, where the D-band (1360 cm−1) and G-band (1590 cm−1) are observed. The D-band is related to defects that originate from imperfections of the aromatic skeleton while the G-band represents the fingerprint of the graphitic crystalline arrangements. The ID/IG ratio is usually used to evaluate the degree of disorder in sp2 hybridized carbon systems and its calculated value equal to 1.18, suggests that GQDs synthesized are almost ordered.

The DLS measurements (see Fig. 5d) performed on dispersions of the GQDs sample in deionized water, showed a single peak at 4.92 nm, confirming the nanosized nature of the carbon material synthesized and the absence of particle aggregation. This was also confirmed by a TEM image of one GQDs particle of 3.4 nm in diameter.

The nanometer size of GQDs was further confirmed by PL analyses. Figure S1 shows the PL spectrum achieved at room temperature under 360 nm excitation coming from the GQDs sample. While the GO sample does not exhibit fluorescence, a strong fluorescence emission peak at 549 nm is observed on the GQDs sample. These findings originated from the photo-Fenton reaction on the very small GQDs nanoparticles [28,29].

The GQDs/SPCE sensor, fabricated by depositing by drop casting the GQDs nanoparticles on the surface of the carbon electrode of SPCE, was used for the electrochemical tests. The successful deposition of GQDs has been investigated by performing ATR-FTIR tests. Compared with the bare SPCE electrode, the ATR-FTIR (see the outcomes in Fig. 6 ) spectrum of GQDs/SPCE is characterized by well-visible bands which can be attributed to the presence of GQDs on the carbon electrode surface.

The characteristic functional groups of GQDs were observed in agreement with the literature [30,31]. According to Fan et al. work, the stretching vibration of −C-O-C- are noticed that are located in our case at 623 cm−1 and 1089 cm−1 while the skeletal vibration of the aromatic ring is noticed at 1583 cm-1 [30,31]. At 1334 cm−1, the alkoxy group is observed [31]. The C-H vibration in the aromatic ring is located at 3060 cm−1 while the C-O bond is noticed at 1408 cm−1 [30,31].

The electrochemical behavior of GQDs/SPCE was evaluated by performing CV and EIS tests in a 10 mM ferrocyanide solution [32]. Fig. 7 a highlights the outcomes of CV studies. Compared with the unmodified SPCE, GQDs/SPCE shows lower [FeCN6]4-/3− faradic current related to the anodic and cathodic peak observed in the CV, but the shift at lower potential and the narrower peak-to-peak separation, demonstrated the good characteristics of the modified electrode.

In Fig. 7b, the Nyquist plot is registered under the conditions described in the experimental section. It is composed of a semi-circle and a linear part. In the inset is shown the modeled equivalent circuit.

Table 1 depicts Randle’s parameters obtained from this study, reporting the electron transfer resistance (RCT) and the electrolyte–electrode surface (Rs) computed for SPCE and GQDs/SPCE sensors., It is noted from the collected EIS data that, when SPCE was modified with GQDs, both Rs and RCT increased only a bit, demonstrating that the electrochemical properties of SPCE were maintained.

To understand more about the sensing mechanism of the modified sensor, a scan rate test ranging from 25 mV/s to 400 mV/s s in the potential range [-0.2 V, 0.8 V] is performed (see Fig. 7c-d). Herein, the increase in scan rate speeds up the electrochemical reaction leading to the linear variation of scan rate vs the current peak showing that the electrochemical reaction is adsorption controlled.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical optimization of GQDs/SPCE for CBD determination
ce:section.ce:para: First, we started to acquire CV data on the electrochemical behavior of SPCE and GQDs/SPCE in the presence of CBD. Based on the literature, the medium nature affects both the current peak and potential of CBD molecules. CBD is not largely soluble in water, so water/ethanol, pure ethanol, or organic solvents were used as a medium for the extraction of this component from the various CBD-containing plants. As we extracted CBD from hemp flour utilizing ethanol, we performed CV in this medium. pH was another important parameter for the CV. López-Iglesias et al. found that their CBD sensor works better in alkaline pH (10) than in neutral and acidic pH [4]. On the other hand, in a recent investigation, Zhang group’s shows that CBD can be determined easily in acidic pH [5]. Thus, it is important to optimize the best conditions for the determination of CBD using the unmodified and modified SPCE electrode. Therefore, we prepared several phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) with different pH (2,4,5, and 10) and carried out DPV tests in the presence of 6.35 µM of CBD (see Fig. 8 ).

As shown, in an alkaline pH medium as well as at pH = 2, both bare and modified electrodes show lower electrochemical performance compared to that noticed at pH = 4 and 5. This result agrees with the reports of previous work [30] and can be explained by the activation of functional groups of GQDs and CBD in mild acidic pH. Thus, due to the better electrochemical performance of GQDs at pH = 5, we selected it as the optimum pH value for the electrochemical CBD testing.

CBD determination was then performed in PBS (0.01 M, pH = 5) at different concentrations ranging from 0 to 6.35 µM. The outcomes of the DPV test are depicted in Fig. 9 . On both electrodes, the electrooxidation of CBD molecules is noticed at ∼ 0.6 V when increasing the CBD concentration to 6.35 µM followed by an increase in the anodic current peak, see Fig. 9a.

A larger variation of anodic current peak (almost double) is indeed observed on the modified GQDs SPCE in contrast with the bare SPCE one. This is due to the improvement of the electrocatalytic process of CBD oxidation on the GQDs-modified electrode surface (see Fig. 10 a). To make hypotheses on the electrochemical oxidation mechanism of CBD, which is a complex molecule having a terpenophenol structure is not simple. Based on the observation that CBD undergoes an irreversible process and that the process is dependent on the pH of the solution, Cincirrione et al. postulated that the electrochemical mechanism of CBD is quite like that of phenols, involving deprotonation and consecutive oxidation of the phenol group(s) [20]. The boosting of the sensitivity towards CBD using the GQDs-modified electrode surface is well noted comparing the calibration curves reported in Fig. 10b.

The sensitivity of GQDs/SPCE (SGQDs/SPCE = 0.98 μAμM-1cm−2) is improved about two times compared with that of the bare (SBare/SPCE = 0.42 μAμM-1cm−2). Important, is also important to determine the limit of detection (LOD) to establish the minimum concentration of CBD that can be reliably detected. This parameter is calculated using the relationship between the standard deviation (SD) and the slope (a) obtained from the calibration curve as follows: LoD = 3.3 × SD/a and it is equal to 0.277 μM.

The results obtained in terms of sensitivity and LOD are superior to those reported by López-Iglesias and colleagues [4]. In this work, Sonogel-Carbon-PEDOT has been proposed as an electrochemical sensor for the electrochemical determination of CBD in a concentration range from 1.59 to 19.1 μM in organic/aqueous buffer solutions, obtaining a high sensitivity of 421 ± 26.1 μA/mM·cm−2 and a low detection limit of 0.94 μM. Table 2 compares results obtained with different sensor devices.

It is known that reproducibility and repeatability are major parameters in the research field to verify the results. Therefore, three different electrodes were prepared and a DPV test for CBD determination was performed at the same experiment conditions, using the same equipment, and method and performed by the same operator. Fig. 11 shows the potential and current peak of the bare and the modified electrodes at 6.4 μM of CBD in PBS (pH = 5) illustrating the good reproducibility. Moreover, the sensitivity is found to be similar to that obtained with the first electrode. Regarding the repeatability, the %RSD is equal to 17.5 at 6.4 μM CBD concentration on the three electrodes.

One critical aspect regarding sensors for these substances is the sensor poisoning with the use, so the surface of the sensor developed should be periodically renewed, in many cases after each electroanalytical assay, and this is high-time consuming. In this respect, the use of disposable screen-printing electrodes, allowing low-cost and easy replacement after the measurement, is an advantage. Thus, the developed GQDs/SPCE sensor is promising for CBD analysis, minimizing the above-mentioned disadvantages commonly found in electrochemical assays with conventional electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical screening of flour seeds sample with GQDs/SPCE
ce:section.ce:para: According to the literature, CBD determination has been performed on different real samples of hemp extracts (flour, oil,..) as well as physiological samples, i.e. human hair, urine, saliva, [5,34–36]. Here, we devoted our attention to the analysis of CBD in a real sample of Hemp seed flour. The extract of this sample was previously characterized by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) by Trovato et al. [37]. Through the analysis of volatile compounds, unsaponifiable fractions, tocopherols, phenolic compounds, and cannabinoids, a long list of phytochemicals were detected and quantified. As expected for the hemp flour, CBD content is very low i. e. 16.9 mg/Kg.
The DPV response recorded with the Hemp seeds flour sample is reported in Fig. 12 a.

The test was carried out by adding different volumes of the Hemp seeds flour extract in ethanol solution. Several peaks were detected in the potential range selected. Increasing the volume of Hemp seed flour extract in the solution to be analyzed, the above peaks increase. The main anodic peak is observed at 0.65 V. Two other additional anodic current peaks are observed at 0.45 V and 0.2 V. These peaks are due to oxidation processes of different components present in the real Hemp sample, as reported for other cannabis or hemp real samples [9,38,39].

Successively, a known quantity of CBD was added to the solution containing the Hemp seeds flour extract. An increase of the current was noted at the expected potential (0.65 V) for CBD oxidation, but the registered variation was much lower than that of the previous one when we added the same CBD quantity in the ethanol solution. The large difference in the calibration curve is shown in Fig. 12b and can be likely related to the strong adsorption caused by the many compounds present in the Hemp seeds flour which led to a poisoning of the sensor reactive sites for the electrocatalytic oxidation of CBD and consequently to a lower capability towards CBD monitoring.

This finding precludes the direct analysis using the calibration curve previously acquired in ethanol solution. Also, the use of the calibration curve obtained in the presence of real Hemp seed samples is questionable; indeed, the strict control of poisoning effects is very difficult because of the large variability of different real hemp samples. In such case, an advantage can be offered by spiking the real sample to be analyzed adding the analyte of interest in a precise and known amount. This method is largely used in analytical chemistry and appears to be suitable for analyzing CBD in the Hemp seeds flour complex mixture (see Fig. 13 ).

By using the spiking method, the amount of CBD present in the analyzed Hemp seeds flour sample was estimated to be 13.7 mg/Kg. Based on the comparison with the CBD quantity determined in this sample by gas chromatography analysis, the value obtained appears to be underestimated. This can be due to the lower sensitivity and to the other factors before discussed which limit the analysis when analyzing complex matrixes like this. The same finding was reported by Cirrincione et al., when performing analyses with the standard addition method by their carbon black-modified glassy carbon sensor [20]. Anyway, the CBD value found in the analyzed Hemp seeds flour was in the same order of magnitude that was truly expected, demonstrating that using the most simple, portable, and cost-effective electrochemical sensor can give a semi-quantitative estimation in fairly agreement with that certified by a conventional analysis carried out by GC.

The use of electrochemical sensors to monitor CBD in real samples is highly desirable as an alternative to more complex conventional analytical methods, such as gas chromatography or high-resolution liquid chromatography [6,7,26]. These latter techniques display good performances in terms of reliability, sensitivity, stability, and reproducibility, and allow to detect and quantification of other components present in real samples. The main drawbacks is because they are generally not suitable for in situ analysis, along with the highly elevated cost and needs of specialized personnel. On the other hand, electrochemical sensors are affordable devices that can easily be set up for industrial mass production. The time required to perform the analysis can be quite short, in the range of a few seconds and, more importantly, having a device able to monitor CBD in a simple way and at reduced cost allows the implementation of control strategies on a large scale. However, the sensitivity of CBD electrochemical sensors is lower compared to laboratory-based techniques. Then, the requested sensitivity, but also selectivity, reproducibility, reliability, and stability, are mandatory aspects to be still reached before proposing them for the CBD analysis in real samples. The applicability of the sensor was demonstrated in this paper for the determination of CBD by the spiking method in hemp seeds flour extract. Our forthcoming objective is to use other CBD-containing real samples.

A screen-printed electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots for the determination of CBD at low concentrations has been developed. The sensitivity of the modified GQDs/SPCE sensor towards CBD in ethanol solution is 0.98 μAμM-1cm−2 with a LoD = 0.277 μM. A linear calibration curve has been assessed in the 0 to 6.4 µM range. The determination of CBD in Hemp seed flour has been also investigated. The results of the present study demonstrate that further study devoted to the optimization of the operative conditions for the electroanalytical test, including an advanced and robust statistical analysis, is needed and could lead to the development of a rapid detection of CBD in real samples.
“This research received no external funding”.
Khouloud Abid: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Angelo Ferlazzo: Supervision, Investigation. Giovanni Neri: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Dr. Trovato and Prof. Paola Dugo of Dip. ChimBioFarAm for supplying the hemp flour sample used in this study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.flatc.2024.100673.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phytocannabinoid pharmacology: medicinal properties of cannabis sativa constituents aside from the “big two”
J. Nat. Prod.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sampson, P.B. Phytocannabinoid Pharmacology: Medicinal Properties of Cannabis Sativa Constituents Aside from the “Big Two.” J. Nat. Prod. 2021, 84, 142–160, doi:10.1021/acs.jnatprod.0c00965.
